title: Emergency Protocols for Supervised Visitation Monitors

sections.title: Why Emergency Preparedness Is Non-Negotiable
sections.content:
Supervised visitation monitors are frequently the only neutral professional in the room when a parent-child interaction takes place. That position carries a profound responsibility: when something goes wrong, there is rarely time to consult, deliberate, or look up a procedure. The seconds after a medical emergency, a credible threat, an abduction attempt, or a natural disaster determine outcomes. Emergency preparedness converts panic into protocol, and protocol into protection.

California Standard of Judicial Administration 5.20 requires that supervised visitation providers be trained in safety and emergency procedures, including the conditions under which a visit must be suspended or terminated and how to coordinate with emergency services. The Supervised Visitation Network (SVN) and CASVSP-aligned curricula reinforce that monitors must not only know de-escalation techniques but also have pre-planned, practiced responses to the full range of emergencies that can occur during a visit.

An emergency, for our purposes, is any situation in which the safety of a child, a parent, a bystander, or the monitor is at immediate risk, or in which the normal conduct of the visit becomes impossible. This includes medical crises, behavioral threats and violence, attempted abduction or custody interference, weapons, fires, evacuations, and weather events. This lesson gives you a structured framework for anticipating, recognizing, and responding to each, and for the documentation and debriefing that must follow.

Throughout this lesson, remember a core principle: in an emergency, child safety comes first, your own safety comes second, and the continuity of the visit comes a distant last. A terminated visit is recoverable. An injury, an abduction, or a death is not.
keyPoints: Emergencies leave no time to improvise — preparedness converts panic into protocol
Standard 5.20 requires training in safety and emergency procedures, including visit suspension
An emergency is any immediate risk to a child, parent, bystander, or monitor
Priority order: child safety first, monitor safety second, visit continuity last
SVN and CASVSP frameworks require practiced, pre-planned emergency responses

sections.title: Categories of Emergency and the Universal Response Sequence
sections.content:
Emergencies during supervised visits fall into recognizable categories, and recognizing the category quickly is the first step toward the correct response. Medical emergencies include a child or adult experiencing a seizure, an allergic reaction, choking, a fall, chest pain, or loss of consciousness. Behavioral and safety emergencies include credible threats, physical aggression, a parent arriving under the influence, the presence of a weapon, or self-harm statements. Custody and abduction emergencies include a parent attempting to leave with a child against the order, an unauthorized person arriving to take the child, or a refusal to return the child at the end of the visit. Environmental emergencies include fire, earthquake, severe weather, power loss, or a facility lockdown.

Regardless of category, monitors apply a universal response sequence that can be remembered as RESPOND: Recognize the emergency and its category; Ensure immediate safety by creating distance between the threat and the child; Summon help by calling 911 or facility security as warranted; Position yourself to protect the child and maintain an exit path; Observe and track what is happening for documentation; Notify your program director as soon as it is safe; Document thoroughly once the immediate danger has passed.

The sequence is deliberately ordered. You do not document while a child is in danger, and you do not call your supervisor before you call 911 for a person who has stopped breathing. The first three steps — Recognize, Ensure safety, Summon help — happen within seconds and often simultaneously. Your training and your facility's written emergency plan should make these reflexive rather than deliberative.

Know your facility before an emergency occurs. Walk the building and locate every exit, the fire extinguishers, the first-aid kit, the AED if one is present, and the designated assembly point. Know which staff are CPR-certified. Keep emergency contact numbers, each child's medical and allergy information, and the relevant custody orders accessible. Preparation done in calm moments is what makes calm action possible in a crisis.
keyPoints: Four emergency categories: medical, behavioral/safety, custody/abduction, environmental
Apply the RESPOND sequence: Recognize, Ensure safety, Summon help, Position, Observe, Notify, Document
The first three steps happen within seconds — they must be reflexive, not deliberative
Never document while a child is still in danger or delay a 911 call to notify a supervisor
Learn your facility's exits, extinguishers, first-aid kit, AED, and assembly point in advance

interactive.question: During a visit, a 6-year-old begins to choke on a piece of candy and cannot speak or cough. The monitor is not certain whether the candy will clear on its own. What is the correct first action?
options: Call the program director to ask whether to intervene
Begin documenting the time and what the child ate
Direct someone to call 911 and, if trained, begin choking-response first aid immediately
Wait two minutes to see whether the child clears the obstruction independently
interactive.explanation: A child who cannot speak, cough, or breathe is in a life-threatening medical emergency. Following the RESPOND sequence, you ensure safety and summon help simultaneously: direct someone to call 911 and, if you are trained, begin appropriate choking first aid at once. Documentation and supervisor notification come only after the immediate danger has passed.

sections.title: When to Call 911 and When to Terminate the Visit
sections.content:
Two of the highest-stakes decisions a monitor makes are when to call 911 and when to terminate a visit. New monitors often hesitate on both, worried about overreacting or appearing to fail. Reframe these as safety decisions, not judgments of your competence. It is always better to summon help that turns out to be unneeded than to withhold it when it was needed.

Call 911 immediately when: a person is unconscious, not breathing, or experiencing chest pain, a seizure, a severe allergic reaction, or uncontrolled bleeding; there is a fire or structural hazard; a weapon is present or brandished; a credible threat of serious violence is made; a physical assault occurs or is imminent; or a child is taken or an abduction is attempted. Do not negotiate with a person holding a weapon and do not attempt to physically pursue someone abducting a child — your role is to be an accurate witness and to direct law enforcement, not to become a second casualty. Provide the dispatcher with your exact location, the nature of the emergency, the number of people involved, descriptions, and whether weapons are present.

Terminate the visit, even absent a 911-level emergency, when safety or the integrity of the order is compromised: a parent makes direct or veiled threats, arrives or becomes impaired by alcohol or drugs, repeatedly violates the order in ways that endanger or distress the child, attempts unauthorized contact or photography, or when a child is in acute distress that cannot be resolved. Use clear, non-negotiable language: 'This visit is ending now. I will document the reason.' Then secure the child, separate the parties, and follow your facility's staggered-departure procedure so that the parties do not encounter each other while leaving.

Termination and a 911 call are not mutually exclusive. Many serious incidents require both: you terminate the visit to remove the child from danger and you call 911 because the situation exceeds what you can safely manage alone. When in doubt, escalate. The cost of an unnecessary call is minutes; the cost of a missed one can be a life.
keyPoints: Reframe calling 911 and terminating as safety decisions, not personal failures
Call 911 for unconsciousness, weapons, credible threats, assault, fire, or abduction
Never pursue an abductor or negotiate with an armed person — be an accurate witness instead
Terminate for threats, impairment, dangerous order violations, or unresolvable child distress
Termination and a 911 call often go together — when in doubt, escalate

sections.title: Case Study: A Medical Emergency Mid-Visit
sections.content:
Monitor Priya was supervising a Saturday visit between Daniel and his 9-year-old son, Marcus, at the KaNun visitation center. The court order noted that Marcus had a severe peanut allergy, and Priya had reviewed his medical information and confirmed the location of his epinephrine auto-injector in the program's first-aid station during her pre-visit preparation.

About forty minutes into the visit, Daniel offered Marcus a granola bar he had brought from home. Before Priya could check the label, Marcus took a bite. Within two minutes, Marcus's lips began to swell, he developed hives on his neck, and he said his throat felt 'tight and weird.' Daniel froze, then began apologizing repeatedly and asking Marcus if he was okay.

Priya recognized the signs of anaphylaxis immediately and applied the RESPOND sequence. She did not waste time confirming the ingredient or reassuring Daniel. She told Daniel clearly, 'Marcus is having an allergic reaction. I need you to step back and let me help him.' She retrieved the epinephrine auto-injector, administered it per her training, and directed a nearby staff member to call 911 and report a child experiencing anaphylaxis with the facility's exact address. She stayed at Marcus's side, kept him calm and upright, and noted the time of the bite, the onset of symptoms, and the time of the injection.

Paramedics arrived within nine minutes and transported Marcus, with the custodial parent meeting them at the hospital. Priya notified her program director by phone as the ambulance departed. Within the hour she completed an incident report capturing the timeline, the food involved, the symptoms observed, the intervention administered, and the agencies notified. Because Priya had prepared in advance — knowing the allergy, locating the auto-injector, and rehearsing the sequence — what could have been fatal became a recoverable crisis. Her documentation later informed the court's decision to add a written 'no outside food' provision to the visitation order.
discussion: Identify the moments where Priya's pre-visit preparation directly enabled her emergency response. What would have happened if she had not located the auto-injector beforehand?
Daniel's instinct was to apologize and seek reassurance from Marcus. Why is it important for the monitor to take direct control rather than allowing the parent to manage the emergency?
What specific elements should Priya's incident report include that a routine visit report would not, and why does the timeline matter so much?
How should Priya handle Daniel emotionally after the ambulance leaves, while still maintaining her neutrality and completing her duties?

sections.title: Building and Executing an Emergency Action Plan
sections.content:
An emergency action plan is only useful if it exists in writing, is reviewed regularly, and is practiced. Before you ever supervise a visit at a facility, obtain and study its written emergency procedures. If the procedures do not exist or are vague, raise this with your program director — gaps in the plan are gaps in safety. Confirm the chain of notification, the location of safety equipment, and the protocol for each emergency category.

Prepare for every individual visit as if an emergency could occur. Review each child's medical conditions, allergies, and medications. Confirm the custody order's provisions, including who is and is not authorized to be present and who may take the child at the end of the visit. Keep emergency contact numbers and the relevant order within reach. Position yourself at the start of every visit with a clear, unobstructed path to an exit and never allow a parent to position between you and the door.

During an emergency, communicate in short, clear, directive sentences. Assign tasks by name when others are present: 'You, call 911. You, bring the first-aid kit.' Diffuse responsibility ('someone call 911') causes dangerous delay because each bystander assumes another will act. Keep the child within your protection and maintain awareness of the threat and the exits at all times. Do not attempt interventions beyond your training; your job is to stabilize, summon, and witness.

After every emergency, three things must happen without exception. First, ensure the child is safe and transferred to the appropriate custodial party or emergency contact, and never released to an unauthorized person even amid chaos. Second, complete thorough documentation within one hour while details are fresh — capture times, direct quotes, observable behaviors, the interventions you performed, and every agency notified. Third, debrief with your supervisor and attend to your own wellbeing, because responding to an emergency is itself a traumatic event for the monitor. Maintain CPR, first-aid, and de-escalation certifications current, and participate in periodic drills so that the RESPOND sequence remains reflexive.
keyPoints: Study your facility's written emergency plan before your first visit and flag any gaps
Prepare per-visit: review medical needs, allergies, custody provisions, and authorized parties
Assign emergency tasks to specific people by name to avoid bystander delay
Never release a child to an unauthorized person, even in the chaos of an emergency
Document within one hour and always debrief — emergency response is traumatic for monitors too
Keep CPR, first-aid, and de-escalation certifications current and practice drills

sections.title: Reflection & Self-Assessment
questions: Mentally walk through the facility where you will work. Can you locate every exit, the first-aid kit, the fire extinguishers, and the assembly point right now? If not, what is your plan to learn them before your first visit?
New monitors often hesitate to call 911 or terminate a visit for fear of overreacting. What self-talk or mental framework will help you act decisively in the moment rather than freeze?

Consider a custody-interference scenario in which a parent attempts to leave with a child. How do you balance the instinct to physically intervene against the guidance to remain an accurate witness and protect your own safety?

Recall a time you faced an unexpected crisis in any setting. How did you respond under pressure, and what does that reveal about the preparation you personally need to perform well during a visit emergency?
After a serious emergency, what specific steps will you take to process the experience and care for yourself, so that the event does not erode your long-term resilience?

summary: This lesson equips supervised visitation monitors with a structured approach to emergencies: recognizing the four categories (medical, behavioral, custody/abduction, environmental), applying the RESPOND sequence, and making decisive calls about when to dial 911 and when to terminate a visit. Grounded in California Standard 5.20 and SVN best practices, it emphasizes pre-visit preparation, clear directive communication, protecting the child above visit continuity, and the documentation and debriefing that must follow every emergency.
